title: Backpressure and flow control
description: When a consumer is slower than a producer, the system must slow the producer (backpressure) or it buffers without bound until it fails.
body: Use when wiring producers to consumers across a network boundary. If a downstream consumer is slower than the producer, something must give: either the producer is slowed (blocking, bounded concurrency, TCP flow control), requests are queued in a bounded buffer, or excess is shed/dropped. With no backpressure the queue grows until memory or descriptors are exhausted and the whole system fails rather than degrading. Do not use unbounded queues as shock absorbers. Actions: bound concurrency and queues, choose a block/drop/shed policy deliberately, and let slowness propagate upstream. Validation signals include flat memory under a slow downstream and the producer rate adapting. Risk boundary: an unbounded buffer converts a transient slowdown into a hard crash and can lose data when it finally fails.
